metadata.career_relevance: Object-oriented programming is THE dominant paradigm in production Java. Every Spring service, every Android app, every backend system is built around classes. Until now, your code has been procedural — you've written methods that take parameters. Real Java code organizes things differently: bundles related data and behavior into classes, then creates objects from those classes. The Inventory Manager from Module 3 used parallel arrays — clunky. Real systems would model Products as a class. This single conceptual shift is the most important leap in your Java journey.

hook.real_world_problem:
Remember the Inventory Manager project from Module 3? Three parallel arrays — productIds, stocks, prices — and the rule that index `i` in all three referred to the SAME product. If you sorted prices, you'd have to sort the other arrays IN SYNC, or your data would mismatch. If you added a new product, you had to update all three arrays. If you wanted to add 'category' or 'discount' fields later, you'd need new arrays everywhere.

That's how procedural code handles 'records' — and it's fragile. The data BELONGS TOGETHER, but the language doesn't enforce that.

Object-oriented programming fixes this. Instead of three parallel arrays, you have ONE array of `Product` objects. Each Product knows its own ID, stock, AND price. Sorting one array sorts the data together. Adding a new field means adding it to the class, period. This is how every modern Java application is structured.

This topic introduces the most important concept in Java: classes. After today, you'll never want to use parallel arrays again.
hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll know how to define a class (a 'blueprint' for objects), create objects using `new`, access fields and methods with dot notation, and work with multiple objects of the same class. You'll refactor the Inventory Manager from Module 3 using a `Product` class — same functionality, dramatically cleaner code. After this topic, you start writing Java the way professionals do.

concept_explanation.intro:
Object-oriented programming has two core concepts:

**Class**: A BLUEPRINT for creating objects. Defines what data the object holds (fields) and what it can do (methods). A class is a TYPE.

**Object**: An INSTANCE of a class. The actual thing you work with. Each object has its own copy of the fields.

Analogy: A 'House' class describes what every house has (walls, doors, address) and can do (open door, lock door). A specific house — say, the one at 123 Main Street — is an OBJECT of type House. There can be many House objects, each with different addresses and states.

You've already used objects: `String` is a class, `"Hello"` is an object. `StringBuilder` is a class. `Arrays.toString(...)` returns a String object. You've been a USER of objects. Now you'll DEFINE your own classes.
concept_explanation.fields_and_dot_notation:
**Fields** (also called instance variables) are variables declared inside a class but outside any method.

```java
public class Person {
    String name;       // field
    int age;           // field
    String email;      // field
}
```

Each object has its OWN copy of each field. Setting `person1.age = 30` doesn't affect `person2.age`.

**Default values** if not initialized:
- numeric types (int, double, etc.): 0 or 0.0
- boolean: false
- char: '\u0000' (null character)
- reference types (String, custom classes, arrays): null

So a freshly-created Person has `name = null`, `age = 0`, `email = null`. **Calling methods on null fields throws NullPointerException** — we'll learn to prevent this in next topic with constructors.

**Dot notation** is how you access fields and methods:

```java
person.name       // read field
person.name = "X"  // write field
person.sayHello()  // call method
```

The pattern: `objectReference.member`. Always works the same way.
concept_explanation.object_references:
**Critical concept**: When you have `Dog d = new Dog();`, the variable `d` doesn't hold the dog. It holds a REFERENCE to the dog — a memory address.

```java
Dog d1 = new Dog();
d1.name = "Rex";

Dog d2 = d1;   // d2 now points to the SAME dog
d2.name = "Buddy";

System.out.println(d1.name);  // Buddy! (same object)
```

This is identical to the array reference behavior from Module 3. Objects are passed by reference.

**Implications**:
- Modifying through `d2` modifies the same object `d1` refers to
- Passing an object to a method gives the method a reference — modifications inside the method affect the caller's object
- Comparing with `==` checks if two variables point to the SAME object, not if their contents are equal

This is why we use `.equals()` for Strings — to compare CONTENT, not REFERENCE. The same will apply to custom classes (covered in Topic 4.4).

**Visual model**:
```
d1 ──┐
      ├──→ [Dog object: name="Buddy", age=5]
d2 ──┘
```

Both variables point to the same object. To create a TRUE copy, you'd need to create a new Dog and copy fields manually (covered later).
concept_explanation.naming_conventions:
Java has strong naming conventions:

**Classes**: PascalCase (first letter capital). `Dog`, `BankAccount`, `OrderProcessor`.

**Fields and methods**: camelCase. `firstName`, `accountBalance`, `calculateTotal()`.

**Constants**: SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE. `static final int MAX_USERS = 100`.

**Packages**: lowercase. `com.codequest.banking`.

Following conventions is REQUIRED in professional Java. Tools, IDEs, and code reviewers assume them. Using `dog` instead of `Dog` for a class will work but mark you as a beginner instantly.

**Class naming rule**: a class is usually a NOUN — something that has data and behaviors. `Dog`, `Order`, `User`, `Calculator`. Not verbs (`Process`, `Calculate`) — those are method names.

**Class names usually match file names**. `Dog.java` contains `public class Dog`. Java enforces this for `public` classes.
concept_explanation.looking_ahead:
**What's coming up in Module 4**:

- **Constructors** (next topic): better way to initialize objects. Instead of `d.name = "Rex"; d.age = 3;`, write `new Dog("Rex", 3)`.
- **Encapsulation**: making fields private, using getter/setter methods. Prevents direct field access (and the bugs that come with it).
- **`this` keyword**: refer to the current object.
- **toString, equals**: methods that make your classes work well with Java's APIs.
- **Composition**: classes that contain other classes (Address inside Person).

Each builds on this foundation. The big takeaway from Topic 4.1: classes bundle DATA and BEHAVIOR. Objects are instances of classes. Each object has its own state. After this, you'll see classes everywhere in Java code.

visual_aid.description: Show a 'Class blueprint' on the LEFT — Dog class with fields (name: String, age: int) and methods (bark()). Right side shows three 'instantiated' Dog objects: Rex (age 3), Buddy (age 5), Luna (age 2) — each as a separate box with its own field values. Arrows from class to each object labeled 'new Dog()'. Below: comparison of parallel arrays vs object array. LEFT: three arrays for names/ages/breeds with index correlation. RIGHT: single array of Dog objects, each self-contained. Annotation: 'Same data, dramatically different organization.'
visual_aid.alt_text: Visual showing Dog class as blueprint creating multiple Dog objects, plus comparison of parallel arrays vs array of objects

worked_example.scenario: Remember the Inventory Manager from Module 3 Topic 3.2? Three parallel arrays for productIds, stocks, and prices. Awkward to maintain. Today we'll REFACTOR it using a `Product` class. SAME functionality. Same data. Dramatically cleaner code. This refactor reveals exactly why OOP exists.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Refactor the inventory system using a `Product` class:
1. Define a `Product` class with fields: `id` (int), `name` (String), `stock` (int), `price` (double)
2. Add an instance method `describe()` that returns a formatted string about the product
3. In main: create an array of 5 Product objects, populate them, print all using describe()

Compare the result to the parallel-arrays version. Notice how much cleaner this is.
worked_example.expected_output:
=== INVENTORY ===
#101 Wireless Mouse — Stock: 45, Price: ₹599.99
#102 USB-C Cable — Stock: 12, Price: ₹249.0
#103 Notebook — Stock: 87, Price: ₹89.5
#104 Mechanical Keyboard — Stock: 8, Price: ₹4500.0
#105 Desk Lamp — Stock: 23, Price: ₹1299.0
things_to_notice: The Product class encapsulates EVERYTHING about a product — id, name, stock, price, AND the describe behavior. Self-contained.
`for (Product p : catalog)` — each iteration gives a Product object, not separate values to recombine.
`p.describe()` accesses the object's own state. Inside the method, `id`, `name`, etc. refer to THAT product's fields.
The verbose initialization (5 lines per product) is annoying. Constructors (next topic) will fix this — eventually we'll write `new Product(101, "Wireless Mouse", 45, 599.99)`.
We can pass a Product to any method that takes a Product parameter. Methods can return a Product. This is real composition — data and behavior together as a unit.
Adding a new field (say `category`) requires changing the class once. With parallel arrays, you'd add an array AND update every loop that touches the data.
Right now, anyone can do `p.price = -999` (set a nonsense price). That's a problem. **Encapsulation** (Topic 4.3) will fix this with private fields and validation.

industry_examples.use_case: REST endpoints work with classes called 'DTOs' (Data Transfer Objects) — User, Order, Product, Account. JSON requests become objects; method calls return objects; objects get serialized back to JSON. Without classes, there'd be no way to organize this data cleanly.

Every concept is a class: Account, Transaction, Customer, Card. A money transfer involves creating a Transaction object, modifying two Account objects, persisting to a database. Imagine doing this with parallel arrays — a nightmare for audits and reliability.

Every UI screen extends a class (Activity, Fragment). Every data model is a class (User, Post, Message). Every adapter, every viewmodel. Classes are the unit of code. Android development without classes is impossible.

Player, Enemy, Weapon, Level — all classes. A combat system uses player.attack(enemy). Inventory holds Item objects. The Tic-Tac-Toe engine from Module 3? It would naturally be a Game class with a board field. Games without OOP are unmaintainable past ~1000 lines of code.

applications.interview_relevance:
OOP fundamentals come up constantly:

- **'What is a class? What is an object?'** — Foundational question. Class = blueprint, object = instance.
- **'Design a class for X'** — Tests if you can identify fields (data) and methods (behavior). Common: 'Design a Library' (Book, Member, Loan classes).
- **'What's the difference between static and instance methods?'** — Tests Module 2 + Module 4 understanding.
- **'Why do we use classes?'** — Expect: organization, reusability, modeling real-world concepts, encapsulation.

System design interviews (senior) are heavily class-based:
- 'Design Uber' — you'd describe Driver, Rider, Trip, Vehicle classes
- 'Design a parking lot' — ParkingLot, Spot, Vehicle, Ticket classes

If you can't reason in classes, you can't pass senior interviews. After Module 4, you'll think in objects naturally.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily life with classes:

- Reading any open-source Java project: you navigate by classes. Each class is one file usually.
- Writing new features: 'Add a refund feature' = create Refund class + method to process it
- Code review: 'This class has too many responsibilities' is the most common feedback at senior level
- Refactoring: extracting cohesive data + methods into a new class is a daily task
- Documentation: JavaDocs describe classes — `@param User user` etc.

IDE features (Eclipse, IntelliJ) are class-centric. Most code navigation is class-based. Once you start writing classes, every part of Java development clicks together better.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Declaring an object reference but not creating an actual object. `Dog d;` doesn't make a dog — d is null.
common_gaps.remediation: To create an object: `Dog d = new Dog();`. The `new` keyword allocates memory and creates the object.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: `Dog[] dogs = new Dog[5]` creates an array of 5 NULL references, not 5 Dogs. Accessing `dogs[0].name` crashes.
common_gaps.remediation: After creating the array, must `dogs[i] = new Dog()` for each slot. The array creation doesn't create the objects.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Trying `Dog.bark()` when bark is an instance method. Requires an object: `d.bark()`.
common_gaps.remediation: Instance methods need an object. ClassName.method() only works for static methods. Create an object first.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compilation error 'non-static method cannot be referenced from a static context'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Doing `Dog d2 = d1` and thinking d2 is a new dog. They're the SAME dog — both variables reference the same object.
common_gaps.remediation: Object assignment shares the reference. To create a real copy, manually create a new object and copy fields.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Making methods static when they need instance data. Then having to pass that data as parameters.
common_gaps.remediation: If a method uses the object's fields, make it an instance method (no static). The data is 'free' — no need to pass.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Verbose methods with many parameters that are really one object's data

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Naming classes in camelCase (`dog`) instead of PascalCase (`Dog`).
common_gaps.remediation: Classes are PascalCase. Always. Java tools and conventions assume this.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Naming the file differently from the public class. `class Dog` in `Animal.java` — compiler error.
common_gaps.remediation: File name must match public class name (case-sensitive). Dog.java contains `public class Dog`.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compilation error: 'class X is public, should be declared in a file named X.java'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Setting `p.price = -100` (nonsense value). Public fields allow any modification.
common_gaps.remediation: This isn't an error YET — but it's a problem we'll solve with encapsulation in Topic 4.3. For now, just be aware: public fields = no validation possible.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Invalid object states reachable from outside

key_takeaways: Class = blueprint, defines what objects of this type look like and can do
Object = instance of a class, created with `new ClassName()`
Fields = variables inside a class, each object has its own copy
Instance methods = methods that operate on a specific object's data (no `static`)
Static methods = belong to the class, callable without an object
Dot notation: `object.field` or `object.method()` to access members
Object references: variables hold POINTERS to objects, not the objects themselves
Object[] is array of references — must `new` each slot
Class names: PascalCase. File name matches public class name.
OOP organizes code as DATA + BEHAVIOR together, not separate arrays of values
Real Java code is built on classes — Module 4 is the most important conceptual leap

self_check_questions.question: What's the difference between a class and an object?
self_check_questions.answer: A class is a BLUEPRINT or TEMPLATE — it defines what fields and methods objects of this type will have. An object is an INSTANCE created from that blueprint, with its own field values. Analogy: 'House' is a class (idea of a house). The actual house at 123 Main Street is an object. You can have many House objects from one House class — each with different addresses, owners, etc.

`Dog d;` declares a Dog REFERENCE but doesn't create a Dog object. `d` is null. Calling any method on null throws NullPointerException. Fix: `Dog d = new Dog(); d.bark();`. The `new Dog()` actually creates the object.

self_check_questions.question: What's the difference between instance methods and static methods?
self_check_questions.answer: Instance methods are called on an OBJECT (`dog.bark()`) and can access that object's fields directly. They have no `static` keyword. Static methods belong to the CLASS (`Math.sqrt(16)`) and can't access any specific object's fields — only their parameters and static fields. Use instance for methods that work with object state; use static for utilities that don't need object state.

`new Dog[3]` creates an array of 3 Dog REFERENCES, all initialized to null. The array exists but contains no Dog objects. `dogs[0].name = ...` throws NullPointerException because `dogs[0]` is null. Fix: `dogs[0] = new Dog(); dogs[0].name = "Rex";`. You must create each Dog separately.

self_check_questions.question: If I have `Dog d1 = new Dog(); Dog d2 = d1; d2.name = "Buddy";`, what is d1.name?
self_check_questions.answer: "Buddy". Object assignment shares references — d2 doesn't get a copy of d1's Dog; it gets a reference to the SAME Dog. Modifying through d2 modifies the same object d1 points to. d1.name and d2.name are the same field of the same object. To make a true copy, you'd need to manually create a new Dog and copy fields, or implement a copy method (Topic 4.x).

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next up: Constructors. The manual field assignment (`d.name = "Rex"; d.age = 3;`) is tedious. Constructors are special methods that run when you create an object — letting you initialize fields in one shot: `new Dog("Rex", 3)`. They also let you ENFORCE that objects are created with valid data. By next topic, your Product class will use a constructor.
